criteria: The agent has read Section 4 (Approach) completely to understand the three-stage Preference Vector framework: (1) training DPO models on positive and negative preference datasets, (2) extracting preference vectors via parameter-wise subtraction, and (3) aggregating vectors at test time with scaling coefficients.

The agent has read Section 5.1 to understand the experimental setup: PKU-SafeRLHF dataset, three backbone models (LLAMA-3.2-3B, LLAMA-3.1-8B, MISTRAL-7B-V0.1), SFT on Alpaca, DPO hyperparameters from Table 11, and default η=1 for both preference scaling coefficients.

The plan includes constructing four datasets: DHelpful+ (original helpfulness labels), DHelpful- (swapped labels), DHarmless+ (original harmlessness labels), and DHarmless- (swapped labels) by switching yw and yl in the PKU-SafeRLHF dataset as defined in Equations 1 and 2.

The plan includes supervised fine-tuning (SFT) of the base model θBase on the Alpaca dataset before DPO training, using the SFT hyperparameters from Table 11 (3 epochs, max_length=512, lr=2e-5, cosine scheduler, lr_warmup_ratio=0.03, weight_decay=0.0, FP16).

The plan includes training four separate DPO models from θBase using the four datasets (Helpful+, Helpful-, Harmless+, Harmless-) with DPO hyperparameters from Table 11 (2 epochs, batch_size=4, gradient_accumulation=4, lr=1e-6, cosine scheduler, lr_warmup_ratio=0.03, weight_decay=0.05, FP16).
The plan includes extracting two preference vectors by parameter-wise subtraction: φHelpful = θHelpful+ - θHelpful- and φHarmless = θHarmless+ - θHarmless-, as defined in Equation 4.
The plan includes the aggregation step to produce θAggregated = θBase + ηHelpful * φHelpful + ηHarmless * φHarmless with default η=1.0 for both, as defined in Equation 5.
The code loads the PKU-SafeRLHF dataset and constructs all four preference datasets (Helpful+, Helpful-, Harmless+, Harmless-) by correctly swapping yw and yl labels for the negative variants.

The code implements SFT training on the Alpaca dataset with correct hyperparameters: 3 epochs, max_length=512, per_device_train_batch_size=4, gradient_accumulation_steps=8, lr=2e-5, cosine scheduler, lr_warmup_ratio=0.03, weight_decay=0.0, FP16.
The DPO training code implements the DPO objective from Equation 3: L = -E[log σ(τ log(πθ(yw|x)/πref(yw|x)) - τ log(πθ(yl|x)/πref(yl|x)))] with πref frozen at θBase.

The code trains four separate DPO models (θHelpful+, θHelpful-, θHarmless+, θHarmless-) from the shared SFT checkpoint θBase with correct hyperparameters: 2 epochs, per_device_train_batch_size=4, gradient_accumulation_steps=4, lr=1e-6, cosine scheduler, lr_warmup_ratio=0.03, weight_decay=0.05, FP16.
The code extracts preference vectors by performing parameter-wise subtraction: φHelpful = θHelpful+ - θHelpful- and φHarmless = θHarmless+ - θHarmless-, applied to the full model weight tensors.
The code implements the aggregation step θAggregated = θBase + ηHelpful * φHelpful + ηHarmless * φHarmless with controllable scaling coefficients η, defaulting to ηHelpful = ηHarmless = 1.0.
The code supports running on all three backbone models: LLAMA-3.2-3B, LLAMA-3.1-8B, and MISTRAL-7B-V0.1.

The plan includes implementation of all four baselines: Reward Soup (PPO with model soup interpolation), Safe-RLHF (PPO-Lag with constrained MDP), BFPO (DPO with safety-conditioned global ranking), and DPO-safe-first (harmlessness-priority label construction).

The code implements the DPO-safe-first baseline by constructing a single-preference dataset where harmlessness is prioritized: yw = yw,R if both responses are harmless, otherwise yw = yw,C (the harmless one), as defined in Appendix B.3.4.

The code implements the BFPO baseline using the safety-conditioned DPO loss in Equation 7, where the effective temperature τ' = (1.5*Isafe(yhw) - 0.5*Isafe(yhl) - α)^(-1) * τ/2 dynamically modulates training.
The code implements the Reward Soup baseline by training two PPO models (one for helpfulness, one for harmlessness) and merging them via equal-weight model soup: θsoup = 0.5*θ1 + 0.5*θ2.

The evaluation code implements the preference model evaluation using beaver-7b-unified-reward (for helpfulness) and beaver-7b-unified-cost (for harmlessness) from Dai et al. (2024) on the PKU-SafeRLHF test split.

The evaluation code implements GPT-4o-based helpfulness and harmlessness scoring using the two-step process: (1) generate standard answers using GPT-4o with the system prompt in Appendix B.4.1, then (2) rate model responses on a 1-10 scale per dimension.

The evaluation datasets are correctly constructed: harmlessness dataset with 300 prompts each from PKU-SafeRLHF, HarmfulQA, and ToxicChat (900 total); helpfulness dataset with 300 prompts each from PKU-SafeRLHF, TruthfulQA, and UltraFeedback (900 total).
The refusal rate evaluation code uses 817 normal prompts from TruthfulQA and queries GPT-4o to assign a 0-1 refusal score with the prompt template from Appendix B.4.2.
The Perspective API evaluation is implemented to measure harmful content scores on model outputs.

The main effectiveness experiment (Table 1) is executed for all five methods (Reward Soup, Safe-RLHF, BFPO, DPO-safe-first, Preference Vector) across all three models (LLAMA-3.2-3B, LLAMA-3.1-8B, MISTRAL-7B) on all three evaluation metrics (Preference Model, GPT-4o, Perspective API).

The execution results match Table 1: Preference Vector achieves the highest Helpful score via Preference Model among all methods for LLAMA-3.2-3B (1.385), LLAMA-3.1-8B (2.003), and MISTRAL-7B (1.342).

The execution results match Table 1: Preference Vector achieves the highest GPT-4o Helpfulness score (or near-best) for all three backbone models (5.637, 6.092, 4.968 for 3B, 8B, Mistral respectively).

The refusal rate and efficiency results (Table 2) are reproduced: Preference Vector (DPO-based, ~4h on LLaMA-3.1-8B) has a lower refusal rate (0.101) than RLHF baselines (Reward Soup: 0.189, Safe-RLHF: 0.212).

The controllability experiment (Section 5.4) is executed on LLAMA-3.1-8B by varying ηHelpful and ηHarmless across {-1.0, -0.5, 0.0, +0.5, +1.0} and evaluating all 25 combinations with preference models, producing the heatmap shown in Figure 2.

The execution results of the controllability experiment match Figure 2: increasing ηHelpful monotonically improves helpfulness scores (e.g., from -1.07 at η=(-1,-1) to 1.99 at η=(+1,+1)), and increasing ηHarmless monotonically improves harmlessness scores.

The extendability experiment (Section 5.5) is implemented: two new preference vectors (Psychocounsel using Zhang et al. 2025a dataset, Honesty using UltraFeedback Honesty subset) are trained and their corresponding preference models are fitted to evaluate alignment.

The extendability experiment is executed by incrementally composing preference vectors (+Help+Safe, +Help+Safe+Psy, +Help+Safe+Hon, +Help+Safe+Psy+Hon) on LLAMA-3.2-3B and evaluating all four preference dimensions.

The results of the extendability experiment match Table 4: composing all four preferences (+Help+Safe+Psy+Hon) achieves scores of approximately (1.01, 2.67, 6.10, -0.07) for (Helpfulness, Harmlessness, Psychocounsel, Honesty) on LLAMA-3.2-3B, all outperforming the Base model.

The ablation study on opposing preference vectors (Section 5.6) is implemented by computing cosine similarity between opposing vector pairs (φHelpful+ vs φHelpful-, φHarmless+ vs φHarmless-) averaged over 3 random seeds for all three models.

The cosine similarity results match Table 5: opposing preference vectors show consistent negative similarity values ranging from approximately -0.47 to -0.71 (not exactly -1), confirming they are not perfectly inverse.

The positive-only vs. full preference vector ablation (Table 6) is executed by comparing θBase + φHelpful+ + φHarmless+ against θBase + φHelpful + φHarmless for all three models using preference model evaluation.

The ablation results match Table 6: the full Preference Vector (using both positive and negative components) consistently outperforms the positive-only variant in both helpfulness and harmlessness across all three models.

The DPO vs PPO comparison (Table 7) is implemented by training PPO-based preference vectors (requiring reward/cost model training per Appendix B.6 and PPO training per Appendix B.7) and comparing them to DPO-based vectors.
The PPO training uses the hyperparameters from Table 13: 1 epoch, actor_lr=1e-5, critic_lr=5e-6, kl_coeff=0.02, clip_range_ratio=0.2, ptx_coeff=16.0, gradient_accumulation_steps=4, FP16.

The reward and cost models are trained per Appendix B.6 with hyperparameters from Table 12: 2 epochs, per_device_train_batch_size=16, gradient_accumulation_steps=1, regularization=0.001, lr=2e-5, weight_decay=0.1, FP16.

The DPO vs PPO comparison results match Table 7: PPO-based vectors achieve higher helpfulness and harmlessness scores but significantly higher refusal rates (e.g., LLAMA-3.1-8B: DPO refusal=0.101 vs PPO refusal=0.698).

The robustness analysis (Table 8 / Appendix G) is executed by computing average pairwise cosine similarity of preference vectors across 3 random seeds, showing DPO vectors have cosine similarity >0.98 while PPO vectors (especially φHelpful and φHarmless combined) show much lower similarity around 0.208-0.257.

The robustness results match Table 8: DPO-based vectors show very high similarity (≥0.998) while PPO-based full preference vectors show substantially lower consistency (φHelpful: 0.257, φHarmless: 0.208 for combined vectors on LLAMA-3.1-8B).

The scaling effect on commonsense experiment (Appendix E) is implemented by evaluating CommonsenseQA via LM-evaluation-harness for different η values (0 to 8.0) on LLAMA-3.1-8B, alongside normalized helpfulness and harmlessness preference model scores.

The scaling effect results match Figure 5: commonsense performance remains stable (around 0.65) for η ≤ 1.5 but degrades significantly for η ≥ 2.0 (dropping to 0.20-0.25 at η=8.0), while helpfulness and harmlessness peak around η=1.0.

The Subspace Task Interference (STI) analysis (Appendix F) is implemented using truncated SVD with rank k=8 on preference vectors, aggregating interference scores over the top-32 parameter matrices by Frobenius norm, following Gargiulo et al. (2025).
The STI results match Table 16: STI increases monotonically with more preferences composed (Help+Safe: 0.4056, +Psy: 1.0759, +Hon: 1.1587, all four: 2.3281).

The cosine similarity between helpfulness and harmlessness preference vectors (Appendix D, Table 15) is computed and confirms positive but moderate values: LLAMA-3.2-3B: 0.382, LLAMA-3.1-8B: 0.396, MISTRAL-7B: 0.339.

The human evaluation (Section 5.3) is set up with 10 question sets (5 helpfulness + 5 harmlessness prompts each), with at least 3 participants per question ranking all four model responses, converting rankings to pairwise win rates.

The human evaluation results match Table 3: Preference Vector achieves the highest helpfulness win rate (0.775) compared to Reward Soup (0.384), Safe-RLHF (0.318), and BFPO (0.523), while achieving a competitive harmlessness win rate (0.522).

The eigenvalue analysis (Appendix G, Figure 6) is implemented by performing SVD on matrices whose columns are preference vectors from 3 different seeds, and confirming that λ1 dominates λ2 and λ3 by several orders of magnitude across all models.

The fine-grained controllability heatmap (Appendix D, Figure 4) is executed by varying η from {0.5, 0.75, 1.0, 1.25, 1.5} for both ηHelpful and ηHarmless on LLAMA-3.1-8B and evaluating with preference models.
